question.text: All other factors remaining constant, what happens to the present value of a future cash flow when the discount rate increases?

answer.text: Present value first increases then decreases

explanation.text: **The Logic:** PV = FV / (1+r)^n. As r increases, the denominator increases, making the PV smaller. Higher discount rate means future cash flows are worth less today because the opportunity cost of money is higher. **The Trap:** This is an inverse relationship — many students confuse the direction. Higher rates make future money less valuable today. **Cross-Exam Context:** The discount rate–PV relationship is fundamental to bond pricing (Ch 9), equity valuation (Ch 8), and all TVM problems. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This explains why bond prices fall when interest rates rise — existing bonds offer lower coupons than newly available rates.
